perturbation.original_text: ii. Licensor will use commercially reasonable efforts to provide approval and/or feedback within five (5) business days after its receipt of a creative submission, or re-submission, with respect to the Licensed Property or marketing materials; provided that: (a) if Licensor declines to approve any submission or re-submission, then it shall provide reasonably detailed feedback in order to enable Licensee to modify the Licensed Property or marketing material accordingly in order to address Licensor's concerns and obtain Licensor's approval, and (b) if Licensor fails to (1) approve or (2) disapprove and provide feedback within such timeframe, then such submission or re-submission is deemed to have been approved. No approval may be unreasonably withdrawn by Licensor once delivered.
perturbation.changed_text: ii. Licensor will use commercially reasonable efforts to provide approval and/or feedback within one (1) business day after its receipt of a creative submission, or re-submission, with respect to the Licensed Property or marketing materials; provided that: (a) if Licensor declines to approve any submission or re-submission, then it shall provide reasonably detailed feedback in order to enable Licensee to modify the Licensed Property or marketing material accordingly in order to address Licensor's concerns and obtain Licensor's approval, and (b) if Licensor fails to (1) approve or (2) disapprove and provide feedback within such timeframe, then such submission or re-submission is deemed to have been approved. No approval may be unreasonably withdrawn by Licensor once delivered.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the approval timeframe to one business day may be unreasonable, especially for complex marketing materials. This could be argued as an unfair business practice.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17200 defines unfair competition as any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice. Requiring approval/feedback on submissions within only 1 business day is an unfair business practice, as it puts unreasonable constraints that can lead to rushed and potentially inadequate review, especially for complex submissions.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the approval timeframe from five business days to one business day. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this shorter timeframe could be unreasonable, and the Law Explanation argues it could be an unfair business practice under California Business and Professions Code § 17200. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines unfair competition as any unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business act or practice. The changed text creates a potential contradiction because the reduced timeframe may be deemed an unfair business practice, rendering the contract terms potentially unenforceable under California law.

perturbation.original_text: d. Reporting. Zynga will, within thirty (30) days of the end of each calendar quarter, commencing with the first full calendar quarter following the Effective Date, furnish WPT with complete statements containing the following information with respect to all Net Revenue from the use of the WPT-brand on the Zynga platform, during the preceding period covered by such statement: the Territory; the amount due WPT (or the remaining unrecouped Annual Minimum Guarantee balance as applicable); Net Revenue; Royalties rate; the distribution channels or portals, the platform, the territory(ies), and itemized Allowable Deductions ("Royalty Statement(s)"). The amount shown to be payable to WPT shall be paid simultaneously with the rendition of the respective Royalty Statement. The statements and payments remitted hereunder shall be delivered to WPT via email to the following email address: Deborah.Frazzetta@wpt.com (ATTN: Deborah Frazzetta, VP, Finance.
perturbation.changed_text: d. Reporting. Zynga will, within sixty (60) days of the end of each calendar year, commencing with the first full calendar year following the Effective Date, furnish WPT with complete statements containing the following information with respect to all Net Revenue from the use of the WPT-brand on the Zynga platform, during the preceding period covered by such statement: the Territory; the amount due WPT (or the remaining unrecouped Annual Minimum Guarantee balance as applicable); Net Revenue; Royalties rate; the distribution channels or portals, the platform, the territory(ies), and itemized Allowable Deductions ("Royalty Statement(s)"). The amount shown to be payable to WPT shall be paid simultaneously with the rendition of the respective Royalty Statement. The statements and payments remitted hereunder shall be delivered to WPT via email to the following email address: Deborah.Frazzetta@wpt.com (ATTN: Deborah Frazzetta, VP, Finance.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting period to annual instead of quarterly could be seen as an unreasonable delay in providing information to WPT, and depending on the context, could be an unfair business practice.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17200 defines unfair competition as any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice. Annual reporting rather than quarterly reporting is an unfair business practice because it unreasonably delays the provision of vital financial data to the other party, which is unfair for the other party to know their financial results.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the reporting period from quarterly to annually. The Law Explanation states that this unreasonably delays the provision of vital financial data, constituting an unfair business practice under California Business and Professions Code § 17200. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines unfair competition as any unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business act or practice. The modification creates a direct contradiction by potentially hindering WPT's ability to monitor and respond to financial performance in a timely manner.

perturbation.original_text: The other party shall have thirty (30) days from the effective date of such notice in which to correct any such default(s) (except those which are not curable), and failing such correction by the end of such thirty (30) day cure period, this Agreement shall thereupon immediately terminate.
perturbation.changed_text: The other party shall have ten (10) days from the effective date of such notice in which to correct any such default(s) (except those which are not curable), and failing such correction by the end of such ten (10) day cure period, this Agreement shall thereupon immediately terminate.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period from 30 days to 10 days for a breach of contract may be considered unreasonably short, especially if complex issues need to be resolved. This could be considered an unfair business practice.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17200 defines unfair competition as any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice. Providing only a 10-day cure period is unfair and may be an unlawful business practice because that timeframe is extremely limited to remedy a default of a commercial agreement, particularly for complex matters.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides 30 days to correct a default, while the changed contract reduces this to 10 days. The Law Explanation states this shorter period may be an unfair business practice in violation of California Business and Professions Code § 17200. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines unfair competition in BPC 17200 as 'any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice'. By reducing the cure period to 10 days, it creates a potential unfair business practice because this timeframe may be inadequate to remedy a default. This directly contradicts the law.
